briefing.opening: Hello everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for 2026-06-28.

sections.title: 🔴 Europe Hit by Heatwaves, Casualties, and Urban Responses
sections.content: Record-breaking heatwaves continue across Europe, once again highlighting the severity of climate change. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), over 1,300 excess deaths related to heatwaves have occurred in Europe [10, 12], directly attributed to the intensifying severity of heatwaves due to climate change [35, 40]. In Germany, Czech Republic, Poland, Hungary, and other regions, casualties have surged amid record temperatures exceeding 40C [42, 43], demonstrating that climate change is no longer solely an issue for polar bears but poses a direct threat to human life and health [6, 37]. In response, the City of London has announced its first comprehensive heatwave response plan, 'Heat Ready London,' which is expected to become a leading example of future urban adaptation policies [13]. However, criticism has also been raised that despite 20 years of heatwave warnings, Europe remains unprepared [8].

sections.title: ⚠️ Climate Change Shaking Agriculture and Fisheries, and Adaptation Efforts
sections.content: Climate change is having severe impacts across the domestic agriculture and fisheries sectors. Pine wilt disease has reached an emergency status due to climate change [2], and fish farms are also struggling, making the urgent promotion of business closure support and restructuring legislation critical [3, 4]. However, amidst this crisis, research into crops resilient to high temperatures and drought is progressing, such as the development of 'K-potatoes' strong against climate change [9], and the potential emergence of nutrient-rich 'climate-resilient crops' is also being raised [1, 5, 39]. Meanwhile, changes in land use, such as the doubling of domestic urban areas and significant reduction in agricultural land and wetlands over the past 30 years [33], are also affecting ecosystems. Furthermore, with one-third of North Korea's forests devastated, there are calls for a reorganization of carbon neutrality strategies through inter-Korean cooperation [14, 18].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Era of Energy Transition: Domestic and International Movements Towards Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Summer electricity consumption has reached an all-time high, further emphasizing the need for a major energy transition and accelerated carbon neutrality [11]. However, issues with power quality due to the intermittency of renewable energy are being pointed out [26, 28], and concerns are being raised that the solar power deployment of only 2GW in the first half of the year could hinder the achievement of the 2030 renewable energy targets [31]. Despite these challenges, efforts towards a carbon-neutral society are continuing in various ways. LH unveiled 'Tomorrow of Carbon Neutrality' at the Land, Infrastructure and Transport Technology Fair [21], and corporate investments are active, with GS E&C pursuing new and renewable energy projects worth 3 trillion won [30]. Furthermore, the Incheon Environmental Corporation has set greenhouse gas reduction targets [24], and grassroots-level practices are spreading as local communities and civic organizations actively conduct carbon neutrality experience activities and environmental cleanup campaigns [16, 19, 20, 22, 23].

sections.title: 📰 Misinformation Spreading Amidst Climate Anxiety and Economic Ripple Effects
sections.content: Climate change is no longer a matter of the 'distant future' but is becoming 'our present' [7], and this is heightening anxiety across society. Amidst this climate anxiety, climate change-related misinformation, such as 'weather hoaxes,' is spreading through social media [32, 36], increasing the importance of access to accurate information. Meanwhile, the climate crisis is leading to increased insurance premiums in the UK economy, causing widespread ripple effects [34, 44], and there are global warnings that ignoring the link between climate change and economic stress is inviting societal risks [38]. Long-term signs of climate change, such as the retreat of the Pine Island Glacier in West Antarctica [45], continue to be observed. It is time for us to accurately recognize the complex threats posed by climate change, guard against information distortion, and forge a societal consensus for immediate action.

briefing.closing: As we have seen in today's briefing, climate change is having immediate and widespread impacts across our lives, economy, and society as a whole. Emphasizing once again that only accurate recognition of the climate crisis and swift action can open the door to a sustainable future, I will now conclude today's climate briefing.

articles.description: Dots (Facts): 'Rain all month' was fake news. Lines (Contexts): Difficult predictions, growing anxiety. Planes (Perspectives): So everyone can avoid 'the rain'. In September last year, when a heavy rain warning was issued for Seoul, a citizen with an umbrella is walking with wet clothes at Gwanghwamun Intersection in Jongno-gu, Seoul. Reporter Han Su-bin. June is coming to an end. Half of the year has already passed. Usually, in mid-to-late June...

summary.phenomenon: A heavy rain warning was issued for Seoul last September, and weather-related fake news, such as 'rain all month,' spread.
summary.cause: The difficulty of weather prediction amidst growing climate anxiety became the backdrop for the spread of fake news.

articles.title: Urban Area Doubles, Farmland and Wetlands Significantly Decrease
articles.original_title: 도시 면적 2배 늘고, 농경지·습지 크게 줄어
articles.description: Ministry of Climate, Analysis of Changes from 1990-2020: 'Domestic Ecosystem Deteriorates Overall.' Over the past 30 years, as the domestic urban area nearly doubled, farmland and wetlands significantly decreased. With the increasing risks of extreme heavy rainfall, wildfires, and landslides, urbanization and the climate crisis are analyzed to have collectively worsened the domestic ecosystem. The Ministry of Climate, Energy, and Environment will publish a report containing trends and future outlooks for Korea's ecosystem...

summary.phenomenon: From 1990 to 2020, over 30 years, the domestic urban area approximately doubled, while farmland and wetlands significantly decreased during the same period.
summary.cause: Urbanization and the climate crisis have complexly interacted, generally deteriorating the domestic ecosystem and increasing the risks of extreme heavy rainfall, wildfires, and landslides.
summary.outlook: The Ministry of Climate, Energy, and Environment is expected to publish a report containing future outlooks for Korea's ecosystem, but specific details of the future outlook are not mentioned in the article.

articles.original_title: Germany, Czechia, Poland and Hungary swelter through hottest days on record
articles.description: Heat records of over 40C set as extreme weather spreads east, with more than 191m in Europe enduring 35C or aboveAfter decades of warnings, why is Europe so unprepared?Germany, Czechia, Poland and Hungary reached record temperatures of more than 40C on Sunday as a heatwave linked to hundreds of deaths in western Europe spread east.More than 191 million people in Europe faced temperatures of at least 35C, with extreme heat warnings across the region. Continue reading...

summary.phenomenon: On a Sunday in July, record-breaking heatwaves exceeding 40C occurred in Germany, Czechia, Poland, and Hungary, with over 191 million people across Europe experiencing temperatures of at least 35C. This heatwave, linked to hundreds of deaths in Western Europe, is spreading eastward.
summary.cause: The article does not specify the exact scientific cause of the heatwave, but it implies a broader climate change context by mentioning Europe's unpreparedness despite 'decades of warnings.'

articles.original_title: Body of boy, 15, recovered from Manchester reservoir amid UK heatwave
articles.description: At least seven people have died in water-related incidents during record-breaking June temperaturesThe body of a 15-year-old boy has been recovered from a reservoir near Manchester, as police renewed warnings about the dangers of swimming in open water during soaring temperatures.Greater Manchester police said officers had been called to reports of a boy getting into difficulty in the water near Cowbury reservoir in Stalybridge at about 6.30pm on Saturday. A body was recovered later that evening

summary.phenomenon: On a Saturday evening during a record-breaking June heatwave, the body of a 15-year-old boy was recovered from a reservoir near Manchester, UK. During this period, at least seven people died in water-related incidents.
summary.outlook: Police have reissued warnings about the dangers of swimming in open water during the heatwave.

articles.original_description: At least seven people have died in water-related incidents during record-breaking June temperaturesThe body of a 15-year-old boy has been recovered from a reservoir near Manchester, as police renewed warnings about the dangers of swimming in open water during soaring temperatures.Greater Manchester police said officers had been called to reports of a boy getting into difficulty in the water near Cowbury reservoir in Stalybridge at about 6.30pm on Saturday. articles.original_title: Rising cost of insuring against climate crisis will have wider knock-on effects for UK economy | Heather Stewart
articles.description: As extreme weather events become more common, economists say government will need to take more active role to protect consumersAnyone attempting to notch up a productive day’s work in the searing heat of southern England this last week was left in little doubt about the impact of extreme weather.But the economic effects of the climate crisis for the UK are not confined to the many hours lost to quietly perspiring – or fetching kids dismissed early from scorching classrooms. Continue reading...

summary.phenomenon: Last week, the scorching heat in southern England led to the loss of productive work hours and early dismissal of students, demonstrating the impact of extreme weather.
summary.outlook: As extreme weather events become more frequent, rising insurance premiums due to the climate crisis are expected to have widespread ripple effects on the UK economy, with economists stating that the government needs to play a more active role in protecting consumers.
